FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 11,875 children ages 9-11 at baseline from 21 U.S. recruitment sites; 4-year longitudinal follow-up with annual assessments

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; prospective longitudinal cohort with quadratic mixed-effect modeling examining relationship between DM usage and brain development (cortex, striatum, cerebellum)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Scientific Reports; published 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (measured via Youth Screen Time Survey) paired with neural individual-trait outcomes: global cortical surface area, striatum volumes, cerebellum volumes

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported: quadratic mixed-effect model coefficients for social media usage interactions with time; Table 2 shows significant interactions (β, p-values) for social media usage × time on cerebellum volumes (p < 0.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: regression coefficients (β), p-values, R² values from model fitting

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design, but social media is used as IV (predictor) in analyses, not exclusively as DV. Design is predictive, not SM-as-DV-only.
notes: Strong paper: isolates social media within broader digital media construct; reports neural outcomes; longitudinal with 4-year follow-up; uses both social media and video game usage as separate IVs

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 10,410 eligible participants; year 1 and year 2 follow-up data from multi-site cohort

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; multivariable logistic regression examining parental monitoring and family conflict (year 1) predicting cyberbullying victimization (year 2)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Academic Pediatrics; published August 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper studies cyberbullying victimization as outcome, with family environment (parental monitoring, family conflict) as predictors. No qualifying smartphone or social media variable measured. Cyberbullying alone without separately-measured SM/phone variable does not meet FT-IC2 per criteria §2.4

notes: Excluded at FT-IC2: no SM/phone variable measured. Family environment is IV; cyberbullying victimization is DV. Does not qualify despite using ABCD data.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data analyzed; uses first five waves of ABCD cohort; Methods section specifies ABCD data from 11,880 youth ages 9–10 at baseline across 21 sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; structural equation modeling (SEM) analyzing direct and indirect effects of traumatic stress exposure on impulse control and sensation-seeking, with deviant peer association as mediator

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Crime & Delinquency; published 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines traumatic stress exposure (IV) predicting impulse control and sensation-seeking (DVs) through deviant peer association (mediator). No smartphone or social media variable is measured or analyzed anywhere in the paper.

notes: Excluded at FT-IC2: no SM/phone variable. Paper focuses on trauma exposure, peer association, and dual-systems cognitive development with no measurement of smartphone or social media use.
